coverage_profile.seniority_signal: Holds senior analyst titles at Macquarie (Senior US Payments and Lifestyle Analyst / Senior US Payments and Digital Commerce Analyst / Senior Analyst) and is a repeat questioner across multiple RCL calls; management addresses him by name and gives substantive answers, consistent with a senior coverage role. Evidence: appears on 2022-Q1, 2022-Q2, 2022-Q3 and 2023-Q1 calls.
coverage_profile.sector_lens: Primary lens is cruise/leisure/travel with a consumer-marketing and digital-commerce tilt (questions about booking curves, itineraries, new-to-cruise, and marketing efficiency). He also brings a payments/digital-commerce lifestyle perspective given his Macquarie remit.
rhetorical_signature: Opener: typically polite—opens with “Thanks/Great/Congrats” and often signals a follow-up (e.g., “I have a follow-up”).
Tends to frame questions as structural vs transitory (asks whether changes are post‑COVID or permanent) and invitations to compare to pre‑COVID norms.
Usually asks concise, targeted two-part questions focused on measurable operating levers (booking window, itinerary mix, marketing spend, labor).

reasoning_style.primary_mode: mixed — blends quantitative-modeling concerns (booking curves, marketing cost per acquisition, SG&A normalization) with qualitative strategic questions (China re‑entry, itinerary/portfolio strategy, Trifecta tradeoffs).
reasoning_style.follow_up_pattern: Frequently asks two-part questions and explicitly signals follow-ups (repeatedly says “I have a follow-up” across 2022-Q1, 2022-Q2, 2023-Q1). He does not often visibly press aggressively when given an answer in the provided history.
reasoning_style.evidence_demanded: Seeks concrete framing and normalization—wants explicit statements about ‘normal’ levels, costs associated with shifts, and mechanisms behind booking curve moves (e.g., asks about costs, structural vs temporary effects, and return to historical booking windows in 2022-Q2 and 2023-Q1).
reasoning_style.anchoring_habits: Anchors questions to pre‑COVID norms and prior company commentary (e.g., repeatedly asks whether trends will ‘return to normal’ and references past guidance/behavior). He also frames questions around company initiatives (Wave, Trifecta) rather than frequently citing peers.

core_topics.what_they_press_on: How close‑in booking trends are evolving and whether the booking window is returning to historical norms; how RCL is managing inventory release and wave strategies. He revisits this topic in follow-ups about Wave and booking cycles.

core_topics.topic: Itinerary mix / new‑to‑cruise and China re‑entry
core_topics.what_they_press_on: Whether shorter itineraries are a post‑COVID tactic or a structural change to drive new‑to‑cruise demand, and longer‑term plans for redeploying ships to China given volatility.
Whether sales & marketing spend is normalizing, whether customer acquisition is getting cheaper/easier, and the structural level of marketing investment relative to TAM and unit economics.
Seeks color on shoreside labor market dynamics and whether employers are seeing wage pressure or tapping international labor as peers reported.
How RCL prioritizes ROIC, EBITDA/margin, deleveraging versus carbon reduction investments and whether carbon efforts carry meaningful near‑term costs or capital.

recurring_concerns.blind_spots: In the provided history he rarely presses on unit pricing/yields, onboard revenue composition, fuel costs/hedging, or detailed capital allocation (share buybacks/dividends). Evidence is limited to these 11 turns, so absence of these topics may reflect the sample rather than his entire coverage universe.
recurring_concerns.stance_drift: Early in 2022 his focus skewed to reopening geography and labor (China re‑entry, shoreside wages in 2022-Q1) and short‑itinerary tactics (2022-Q2). By mid‑2022 he shifted toward marketing efficiency and longer‑term strategic tradeoffs (SG&A normalization in 2022-Q3; Trifecta/ROIC tradeoffs in 2022-Q3). In 2023 he returned attention to booking‑curve and inventory/revenue‑management questions (2023-Q1). Evidence for this drift is limited to the calls listed (2022-Q1 through 2023-Q1).
